my_thoughts: The relativity/definition works: will = relative (mechanical will = struggle result, not absolute), introduces: relativity (not absolute, but relative). The student challenges definition (challenging definition of will), which shows they understand: no will = absolute (definition challenge). They'll likely ask about protocol next. Hypothesis: definition challenges trigger protocol questions—testable by tracking whether definition challenges lead to protocol inquiries.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student challenges definition ('You said no will was possible for us?')—challenging definition of will via conceptual inquiry and definitional seeking. They understand: no will = absolute (definition challenge). The comparison_contrast mechanism defines: will = relative (mechanical will = struggle result, not absolute), introduces: relativity (not absolute, but relative). Pattern: definition challenge → relativity/definition → [next: protocol question]. Low confidence because definition challenges can lead to clarification requests or compliance.
visible_response:
Will is a relative term: there are different wills on different levels.
A mechanical man who never thinks of development has only a multitude of small
wills that are quite mechanical.
He has a certain desire: one side of him wants to do
something and another side is afraid he will be punished if he does it.
A struggle ensues between the different tendencies and the result of this struggle we
call 'will'.
